meta.geometry_note: All sections MCQ only. Questions cover faces/edges/vertices, Euler's formula, nets, views and cross-sections of 3D shapes.

questions.text: Euler's formula for any polyhedron is:
questions.explanation: Euler's formula: Faces + Vertices = Edges + 2, i.e., F + V = E + 2.

questions.text: A cube has:
questions.explanation: A cube has 6 square faces, 12 edges and 8 vertices. Euler check: 6+8=14=12+2 ✓.

questions.text: The top view of a cone is:
questions.explanation: When a cone is viewed from the top, the circular base is visible with a point (apex) at the centre — the overall outline is a circle.

questions.text: Which of the following is NOT a polyhedron?
questions.explanation: A polyhedron has only flat (polygonal) faces. A cylinder has curved surfaces — it is NOT a polyhedron.

questions.text: A triangular prism has:
questions.explanation: A triangular prism: 2 triangular faces + 3 rectangular faces = 5 faces. 9 edges, 6 vertices. Check: 5+6=11=9+2 ✓.

questions.text: The front view of a cylinder (standing upright) is:
questions.explanation: Looking at an upright cylinder from the front, you see a rectangle (height × diameter).

questions.text: A square pyramid has a square base and 4 triangular faces. How many edges does it have?
questions.explanation: Square pyramid: 4 base edges + 4 lateral edges = 8 edges. Faces = 5, Vertices = 5. Check: 5+5=10=8+2 ✓.

questions.text: In the figure, a net is shown. Which solid does it form when folded?

options.a: A net with 6 squares forms a cube
options.b: A net with 6 squares forms a cuboid
options.c: A net with 6 squares forms a cylinder
options.d: A net with 6 squares forms a cone

questions.explanation: A net consisting of 6 equal squares, when folded, forms a cube (all faces equal).

questions.text: The number of vertices of a tetrahedron (triangular pyramid) is:
questions.explanation: A tetrahedron has 4 triangular faces, 6 edges and 4 vertices. Check: 4+4=8=6+2 ✓.

questions.text: When a sphere is cut by a plane, the cross-section is always:
questions.explanation: Any plane cross-section of a sphere is always a circle (or a point if the plane is tangent).

questions.text: The top view of a cuboid is:
questions.explanation: The top view of a cuboid (viewed from directly above) is a rectangle — the outline of the top face.

questions.text: In the figure, a net of a cylinder consists of:
questions.explanation: The net of a cylinder: 2 circular faces (top and bottom) + 1 curved surface (rectangle when unrolled).

questions.text: A regular polyhedron has faces that are:
questions.explanation: A regular polyhedron (Platonic solid) has all faces as congruent regular polygons with the same number of faces meeting at each vertex.

questions.text: If a solid has F = 7, V = 10, what is E using Euler's formula?
The side view of a cone (viewed from the side) is:
questions.explanation: The side view of a cone is an isosceles triangle — the apex at top, base as the base of the triangle.

questions.text: When a cube is cut by a plane parallel to its base, the cross-section is:
questions.explanation: A plane parallel to the base of a cube cuts it to produce a square cross-section (same shape as the base).

questions.text: In the figure, the front view of a building is a rectangle with a triangle on top. This represents:

options.a: A cube with a cone on top
options.b: A cuboid with a triangular prism roof
options.c: A cylinder with a cone on top
options.d: A sphere with a pyramid on top

questions.explanation: A front view showing a rectangle (walls) with a triangle on top (roof) represents a cuboid with a triangular prism (the sloped roof).

questions.text: The number of faces of a pentagonal prism is:
questions.explanation: A pentagonal prism: 2 pentagonal faces + 5 rectangular faces = 7 faces. Edges=15, Vertices=10. Check: 7+10=17=15+2 ✓.

questions.text: Which of the following nets will NOT form a cube?

options.d: A straight line of 6 squares in a row

questions.explanation: 6 squares in a straight line cannot fold into a cube — opposite faces would overlap. The other shapes (cross, L, T) are valid cube nets.

questions.text: The base of a prism and its cross-section parallel to the base are always:
questions.explanation: In a prism, all cross-sections parallel to the base are congruent to the base — this is a defining property of prisms.

questions.text: A polyhedron has 12 edges and 8 vertices. How many faces does it have?
questions.explanation: F + V = E + 2. F + 8 = 12 + 2 = 14. F = 6 faces. (This is a cube/cuboid.)

questions.text: In the figure, a solid is formed by stacking a cube (side 4 cm) below a square pyramid (base 4 cm). The front view of this combined solid is:

options.c: A rectangle with a triangle on top

questions.explanation: The front view: cube appears as a square/rectangle, pyramid above appears as a triangle. Combined front view = rectangle with triangle on top.

questions.text: A cube is cut diagonally (the cutting plane passes through 4 vertices). The cross-section formed is:
questions.explanation: A diagonal cut through a cube passing through 4 vertices creates a rectangular cross-section.

questions.text: In the figure, three cubes of side 1 cm are placed side by side in a row. What is the front view?
questions.explanation: Three unit cubes in a row: front view is 3 cm wide and 1 cm tall = a 1×3 rectangle.

questions.text: A solid has 20 faces, all of which are equilateral triangles, and 12 vertices. How many edges does it have?
questions.explanation: F + V = E + 2. 20 + 12 = E + 2. E = 30. This is an icosahedron.

questions.text: In the figure, a cylinder is placed on top of a cube. The top view (looking from above) of this combined solid is:
questions.explanation: From the top, the cylinder's circular base is visible within the square top face of the cube. View = circle inside a square.

questions.text: A triangular prism stands on its triangular base. Its top view is:
questions.explanation: The top view of a triangular prism standing on its triangular base is the triangular top face — a triangle.

questions.text: In the figure, a cone is placed with its apex pointing down and base facing up. The front view is:
questions.explanation: With apex pointing down, the front view of the cone is an inverted triangle — wide at the top (base) and pointed at the bottom (apex).

questions.text: A cuboid (not a cube) has length 6 cm, width 4 cm and height 3 cm. How many faces does it have?
questions.explanation: A cuboid always has 6 rectangular faces (3 pairs of opposite faces). Edges=12, Vertices=8. Euler: 6+8=14=12+2 ✓.

questions.text: In the figure, 4 unit cubes are arranged in an L-shape (3 cubes in a row, 1 cube placed on top of the leftmost). What is the front view?

options.b: A 2×1 rectangle on the left and 1×2 rectangle on the right
options.c: An L-shape (2 units tall on left, 1 unit tall on right)

questions.explanation: 3 cubes in a row (1 unit tall each) + 1 cube on top of the leftmost (making 2 units tall on left). Front view: 2 units tall on left, 1 unit tall for the other 2 = an L-shape.

questions.text: A polyhedron has F faces, V vertices and E edges. If E = 2F = 2V, which type of polyhedron is this?
questions.explanation: E=2F and E=2V → F=V=E/2. Euler: F+V=E+2 → E/2+E/2=E+2 → E=E+2, impossible! Wait: let E=6, F=3, V=3: F+V=6=E+2=8 ✗. Try E=6,F=4,V=4: 4+4=8=6+2=8 ✓. But E=2F means 6=8 ✗. Reconsidering: tetrahedron: F=4,V=4,E=6. E=2F means 6=8 ✗. Actually no solid satisfies E=2F=2V. But closest: tetrahedron has F=V=4, E=6. 2F=8≠6=E. The answer 'd' (cannot exist) seems right. Let me reconsider: E=2F=2V → F=V and E=2F. Euler: F+F=2F+2 → 2F=2F+2 → 0=2. Impossible. Answer is d.

questions.text: In the figure, a solid is made by placing 4 unit cubes: one at base, two on top of it side by side, one on top of the left of the second layer. The side view (from the right) shows:
questions.explanation: The side view (from the right) shows only what is visible from the right side. The rightmost column visible is 2 cubes tall (base + one directly above). Side view = 1×2 rectangle (2 units tall, 1 unit wide).

questions.text: A cuboid has dimensions 3 cm × 2 cm × 4 cm. When cut by a plane parallel to the 3×2 face, the cross-section has area:
questions.explanation: A plane parallel to the 3×2 face gives a cross-section identical to that face: area = 3×2 = 6 cm².

questions.text: In the figure, a solid has the following views: Front view = triangle, Side view = triangle, Top view = square. The solid is most likely:
questions.explanation: Front view = triangle ✓ (pyramid). Side view = triangle ✓ (pyramid). Top view = square ✓ (square base viewed from top). → Square pyramid.

questions.text: A cube of side 3 cm is painted red on all faces and then cut into 27 unit cubes. How many unit cubes have exactly 2 faces painted?
questions.explanation: Unit cubes with exactly 2 painted faces are the edge cubes (not corner). A 3×3×3 cube has 12 edges, each contributing 1 such cube (the middle of each edge). Total = 12.

questions.text: In the figure, a map shows a road from point A running east for 3 cm, then north for 4 cm to point B. What is the straight-line distance AB on the map (using scale 1 cm = 1 km)?
questions.explanation: East 3 cm, North 4 cm → right triangle. AB = √(3²+4²) = √(9+16) = √25 = 5 cm on map = 5 km actual.

questions.text: A solid has 8 faces, 12 edges. How many vertices does it have using Euler's formula?
A pentagonal pyramid has a pentagonal base and 5 triangular faces. How many edges does it have?
questions.explanation: Pentagonal pyramid: F=6 (1 pentagon + 5 triangles), V=6 (5 base + 1 apex). E = F+V-2 = 6+6-2 = 10.

questions.text: In the figure, 8 unit cubes are arranged to form a 2×2×2 cube. How many unit cubes are NOT visible from outside?
questions.explanation: In a 2×2×2 arrangement, all 8 unit cubes are on the outer surface — every cube can be seen from at least one direction. None are completely hidden.

questions.text: A hexagonal prism has a hexagonal base with 6 sides. How many faces, edges and vertices does it have?
questions.explanation: Hexagonal prism: 2 hexagonal faces + 6 rectangular faces = 8 faces. 6+6+6=18 edges. 6+6=12 vertices. Check: 8+12=20=18+2 ✓.

questions.text: In the figure, a cube of side 4 cm is cut into smaller cubes of side 1 cm. How many smaller cubes are obtained?
questions.explanation: Number of smaller cubes = (4÷1)³ = 4³ = 64 unit cubes.

questions.text: In the figure, a 3×3×3 cube is made of unit cubes. How many unit cubes have exactly 3 faces painted (corner cubes)?
questions.explanation: Corner cubes have 3 painted faces. A 3×3×3 cube has 8 corners. So 8 unit cubes have exactly 3 faces painted.

questions.text: How many unit cubes in a 3×3×3 painted cube have exactly 0 faces painted (completely inside)?
questions.explanation: The innermost cube(s): in a 3×3×3, the centre cube is completely surrounded — 1 unit cube has 0 faces painted.

questions.text: A map uses a scale of 1 cm : 200 m. Two places appear 5.5 cm apart on the map. What is their actual distance?
questions.explanation: Actual distance = map distance × scale = 5.5 × 200 = 1100 m.

questions.text: The net of a cube has 6 square faces. Each square has side 3 cm. What is the total surface area of the cube?
questions.explanation: Surface area of cube = 6 × side² = 6 × 3² = 6 × 9 = 54 cm².

questions.text: In the figure, a solid has front view = rectangle (3×2), side view = rectangle (2×2) and top view = rectangle (3×2). What type of solid is it, and what are its dimensions?
questions.explanation: Front view 3×2 → length=3, height=2. Side view 2×2 → width=2, height=2. Top view 3×2 → length=3, width=2. Solid = cuboid 3×2×2.

cases.case_text: An architect designs a children's play area. She uses the following solid shapes: (i) A cylindrical tunnel — radius 1 m, length 3 m. (ii) A cube-shaped sandpit — side 2 m. (iii) A square pyramid slide — base 2m × 2m, 4 triangular faces. She draws the front view, top view and side view of each shape for her blueprint.

subparts.text: The front view (from the side of the opening) of the cylindrical tunnel is:
subparts.explanation: Looking at a cylinder from the end (front of tunnel opening), you see the circular base — a circle.

subparts.text: The top view of the square pyramid slide is:
subparts.explanation: The top view of a square pyramid: the square base is visible, and the 4 edges from base corners to apex project as diagonals. View = a square with diagonals.

subparts.text: For the square pyramid slide (F=5, V=5), how many edges does it have?

cases.case_text: A teacher gives students a 3×3×3 cube made of 27 unit cubes. All outer faces of the large cube are painted blue. Students are asked to answer questions about the paint on the unit cubes.

subparts.text: How many unit cubes have exactly 1 face painted (face cubes — not on edges or corners)?
subparts.explanation: Face cubes (centre of each face): a 3×3×3 cube has 6 faces, each with 1 central unit cube (not on any edge). Total = 6 unit cubes with exactly 1 face painted.

subparts.text: How many unit cubes have exactly 2 faces painted (edge cubes)?
subparts.explanation: Edge cubes (middle of each edge of the large cube): a cube has 12 edges, each edge of the 3×3×3 has 1 middle unit cube. Total = 12 unit cubes with exactly 2 faces painted.
8+12+6+1 = 27 unit cubes in total. This accounts for all 27 cubes in the 3×3×3 arrangement ✓.
